title: Resources
text:
// CORE TECHNOLOGY  ·  核心技术
Everything your team needs to build an AI-powered robot. Every team gets the same foundation — what you build on top of it is entirely yours.

title: Resources
text:
Hardware

ESP32-Based Boards
The ESP32 is the brain of your robot: dual-core 240 MHz processor, built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, and enough compute for real-time AI interaction.
We use ESP32-based development boards (e.g. ESP32-S3, ESP32-CAM variants depending on your project).
Specifications relevant to this course: - Dual-core Xtensa LX7 @ 240 MHz - 512 KB SRAM + up to 16 MB Flash (module-dependent) - Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n (essential for LLM API calls) - I2C, SPI, UART, PWM, ADC — all the interfaces you’ll need
Resources: - Official ESP32 Datasheet - ESP-IDF Documentation - PlatformIO ESP32 platform


Peripherals
Teams will work with a subset of these components depending on their concept:



Component
Purpose
Interface




OLED display (SSD1306, 128×64)
Text / emotion display
I2C


LCD display (ST7789, 240×240)
Color display
SPI


Microphone (INMP441)
Voice input
I2S


Speaker + amplifier (MAX98357)
Voice output
I2S


Camera (OV2640)
Visual awareness
SCCB/I2C + parallel


Servo / DC motor + driver
Movement, expression
PWM


Ultrasonic sensor (HC-SR04)
Distance / presence
GPIO


Capacitive touch sensor
Touch interaction
GPIO/ADC



3D Fabrication:
Your robot’s enclosure will be 3D-modeled and printed. The physical form is part of the design — it communicates personality before the robot says a word.
Recommended modeling tools: TinkerCAD (beginner), Fusion 360 (advanced, free for students).

title: Resources
text:
Frameworks

ESP-Claw
ESP-Claw is a purpose-built firmware framework for ESP32 AI robots. It abstracts the complex integration of voice, display, and LLM calls so you can focus on your idea rather than low-level plumbing.
What ESP-Claw handles for you: - Wi-Fi connection management - HTTP/HTTPS requests to LLM APIs - Audio capture and playback pipeline - Display rendering (text, icons, animations) - OTA firmware updates
Espressif engineers will give a guest lecture on ESP32 hardware and the ESP-Claw framework in Week 1.
→ ESP-Claw Documentation


OpenRouter
OpenRouter is a unified API gateway for large language models. Instead of integrating each model vendor separately, you write one API call and can switch between Qwen, DeepSeek, Claude, and dozens of others by changing a single parameter.
Why this matters for hardware: - Your ESP32 speaks to one endpoint — simpler networking code - Test multiple models without re-engineering your firmware - Manage costs across models in one dashboard - Automatic fallbacks if a model is unavailable
# Example: same call, different model
curl https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENROUTER_KEY" \
  -d '{"model": "qwen/qwen-2.5-72b-instruct", "messages": [...]}'

# Switch to DeepSeek: change one field
  -d '{"model": "deepseek/deepseek-chat", "messages": [...]}'
→ OpenRouter Documentation · Model list & pricing

title: Resources
text:
Large Language Models

Models we’ll work with
Your robot’s intelligence comes from LLMs. We’ll use models accessible via OpenRouter:








Model
Strengths
Use when




Qwen 2.5
Excellent Chinese + English, fast
Bilingual interactions, speed matters


DeepSeek Chat
Strong reasoning, very cost-effective
Logic-heavy interactions, budget-conscious


Claude (Haiku / Sonnet)
Best instruction-following, nuanced responses
Complex personality, careful tone



Choosing a model for your robot: - Response speed matters on embedded hardware — test latency with your Wi-Fi - Cost matters if your demo runs many queries — check OpenRouter’s per-token pricing - Language quality matters for personality — do a side-by-side with your system prompt






TipModel selection isn’t permanent



Your system prompt and model choice are both adjustable throughout the course. Design your firmware so swapping models is a one-line change.

title: Resources
text:
AI Coding Tools
Writing embedded firmware is faster with AI assistance. You will use:



Claude Code
Anthropic’s AI pair programmer in your terminal. Understands your full codebase and can write, debug, and explain C++ / Arduino / ESP-IDF code.
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
→ Claude Code docs




GitHub Copilot / Codex
In-editor code completion and generation. Excellent for boilerplate, API integration, and explaining unfamiliar code.
→ GitHub Copilot for students (free)

title: Resources
text:
Version Control
All teams must maintain a GitHub repository throughout the course.
# Suggested repo structure
your-robot/
├── firmware/          # ESP32 code
│   ├── src/
│   └── platformio.ini
├── design/            # 3D files, schematics, mockups
├── research/          # User interview notes, concept brief
├── poster/            # Poster source file
└── README.md          # Project description + demo photo/video






Note



Your GitHub repo is a deliverable. Commit regularly. Write commit messages that would make sense to a stranger.

title: Week 3 — Build
text:
// WEEK THREE  ·  第三周
  Build
  制造
  
    The pieces exist. Now you make them into a product.
    Week 3 is the most intense week: soldering, firmware integration, 3D printing, and relentless user testing.
    By Friday you should have a working robot in an enclosure.

title: Week 3 — Build
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of Week 3 you will be able to:

Integrate all hardware components (sensors, display, motor/speaker) into a unified system
Print and assemble a 3D-modeled enclosure
Run structured user tests and incorporate feedback into the product
Use Claude Code, Codex, or other AI tools to accelerate firmware development
Prepare a working demo for Week 4

title: Week 3 — Build
text:
Sessions

Session 7 — Full Hardware Integration
Everything on the breadboard; make it work together.
Agenda: - Solder your circuit (move off the breadboard if components are confirmed) - Integrate all sensors and actuators: camera, motor, speaker, display - Firmware: handle all hardware in one ESP-Claw sketch - Stress test: run continuously for 10 minutes — what breaks? - Bug triage with instructors






WarningThe integration wall



Most teams hit a wall here — something that worked separately breaks when combined. This is normal. Budget 50% of this session for debugging. The debugging is the learning.


Common integration issues to watch for:



Symptom
Likely cause




Device crashes on startup
Power rail can’t supply enough current


Display garbled
I2C address conflict


LLM calls time out
Wi-Fi and heavy compute conflict


Motor causes resets
Motor driver needs separate power






Session 8 — 3D Print & Assemble
Your robot gets a body.






TipDesign for printability



The best enclosure is the one that actually prints. Design with 0.2mm layer height in mind, avoid overhangs greater than 45°, and add clearance for USB ports and cables.


Agenda: - Finalize enclosure design in Fusion 360 / TinkerCAD / Blender - Submit print jobs (prints typically take 3–8 hours) - While printing: refine firmware, document wiring - Assemble when print is complete: mount board, route cables, close enclosure - Reality check: does it still feel like your concept brief?
3D modeling tools:



Tool
Best for
Free?




TinkerCAD
Simple shapes, beginners
✅ Yes


Fusion 360
Parametric design, precise fits
✅ Student/edu


Blender
Organic/sculpted shapes
✅ Yes


SolidWorks
Professional mechanical design
❌ License needed






Session 9 — User Testing & Iteration
Put the product in front of real people. Fix what matters.






NoteStructured user testing



A good user test takes 15 minutes per person. Give them a task, watch silently, take notes. Don’t explain or defend — just observe.


Agenda: - Each team runs 3–4 user tests (find people outside the class) - Observe: what confuses people? What delights them? What do they ignore? - Debrief: what are the top 3 things to fix before Week 4? - Sprint: make the 3 fixes (if more than a day of work, scope them down) - Final hardware check with instructor sign-off
What to test: - Can a new user figure out how to interact with the robot without explanation? - Does the robot’s personality come through clearly? - Is the physical form coherent with the personality? - Does it work reliably for 5 minutes straight?


✓ Week 3 Deliverables

Working robot in enclosure — fully integrated, reliable, battery/USB powered
User test report — 3–4 testers, observations, top changes made
Source code — commented, committed to GitHub
Wiring diagram — documented schematic
Demo-ready — 2-minute rehearsed demo of the robot’s key interaction

title: Week 3 — Build
text:
Resources for Week 3

ESP-Claw hardware integration guide — multi-component examples
Claude Code — AI pair programmer in your terminal
TinkerCAD — web-based 3D modeling (free)
Fusion 360 for students — professional-grade free license
Prusa Slicer — open source slicer for most printers


← Week 2 — Design  |  Week 4 — Pitch It →

title: Week 1 — Conceive
text:
// WEEK ONE  ·  第一周
  Conceive
  构思
  
    Before you touch a single component, you need to understand the person your robot will serve.
    Week 1 is about listening, asking, and imagining — turning a vague idea into a focused concept.

title: Week 1 — Conceive
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of Week 1 you will be able to:

Conduct basic user research interviews and synthesize findings
Define a target user and a genuine unmet need
Articulate the personality and purpose of your robot in a one-page concept brief
Sketch interaction scenarios and rough form factors
Present your concept for peer critique and iteration

title: Week 1 — Conceive
text:
Sessions

Session 1 — Kick-off & Team Formation
The course begins. Teams are formed across disciplines — you may not know your teammates yet, and that’s by design.






Note



What happens: Course introduction, team assignments, overview of the 4-week arc. First look at the ESP32 hardware and ESP-Claw framework.


Agenda: - Course overview and grading (Pass / No Pass) - Meet your team — skills mapping exercise - Hardware unboxing: ESP32 board, sensors, displays - Guest overview: Espressif introduction to ESP-Claw - Assignment: individual user interview before Session 2



Session 2 — User Research & Empathy
You’ve each spoken to a potential user. Now you share what you heard.






TipDesign Thinking in action



The goal of user research is not to confirm your assumptions — it’s to break them. The most interesting insights come from unexpected answers.


Agenda: - Share interview findings in your team (5 min each) - Affinity mapping: cluster insights into themes - Identify the top 2–3 user needs worth solving - Define your target user persona - Sketch 3 possible robot concepts (rough, fast, no judgment)



Session 3 — Concept Selection & Personality Brief
Your team converges on one direction.
Agenda: - Dot voting: which concept is most feasible and most meaningful? - Concept refinement: what does the robot do, say, and feel like? - Write the Robot Personality Brief (see deliverable below) - Technical feasibility check with instructors - Peer critique round: 3-minute pitches, feedback cards

title: Week 1 — Conceive
text:
The Robot Personality Brief
This is the key output of Week 1. It answers these questions in one page:







Question
What to define




Who is it for?
Specific user + context


What problem does it solve?
The core unmet need


What does it feel like to interact with?
Personality adjectives (3–5)


What does it say / how does it speak?
Tone, language, style


What does it look like?
Sketch or moodboard


What does it not do?
Explicit non-goals









ImportantKeep it on one page



The constraint is intentional. If you can’t summarize your concept in one page, you don’t have a concept yet — you have a wish list.




✓ Week 1 Deliverables

User Persona — one documented user with a real need (from your interview)
Robot Concept Brief — 1-page: user, need, personality, rough form factor
3 concept sketches — rough drawings, one per team member
Team roles defined — who leads design, firmware, fabrication, pitch

title: Week 1 — Conceive
text:
Resources for Week 1

Stanford d.school Design Thinking Bootcamp Bootleg — the canonical guide to empathy and concept work
IDEO Human-Centered Design toolkit — interview and synthesis methods
ESP-Claw documentation — start getting familiar early


Next: Week 2 — Design →

Guest Lecturers

      in the context of AI-powered physical products.

title: MAKER-X
text:
Summer 2026  ·  ShanghaiTech University
    
    
    
      School of Creativity & Art  ·  SIST  ·  Kedao College
    
    
    
      You don't just build a robot — you conceive the idea,
      design its personality, build the electronics, and
      pitch it.
      Four weeks. One team. One product you own end-to-end.
    
    
      你不只是在制造机器人——你要构思创意、设计个性、搭建电路，最终登台展示。
    
    ✉  I Am Interested

title: MAKER-X
text:
The Mission · 课程使命
Maker-X is an intensive 4-week project course where cross-disciplinary teams build AI-powered robotic companions from scratch — hardware, firmware, personality, and pitch. Instruction in Chinese & English.



01 ### Conceive Identify a real user and a genuine need. Define the personality and purpose of your robot before touching any hardware.




02 ### Design Shape, sound, language, expression — every detail of the robot’s soul. Prototype rapidly and iterate without fear.




03 ### Build Solder, code, 3D-print, and integrate. Electronics, firmware, enclosure — one complete, working product.




04 ### Pitch It ★ Present your product to real university guests. A working demo, a poster, a story — your public debut.

title: MAKER-X
text:
Course Objectives · 课程目标
Maker-X is about more than building a robot. It’s about becoming someone who can think, collaborate, and create at the intersection of technology and design.



🤝 Cross-discipline Collaboration
Work in teams spanning engineering, design, and beyond. Learn to communicate across disciplines and ship something greater than the sum of its parts.




🧠 Confidence with AI & LLMs
Go from curious to capable. You’ll integrate large language models into real hardware — learning to prompt, tune, and deploy AI as a core part of a product.




🎨 Design Thinking in Practice
Empathy, creativity, rapid prototyping, iteration, and communication — not as theory, but as daily practice. The product is the proof.

title: MAKER-X
section: Who Should Join · 招生对象
text:
Who Should Join · 招生对象



🎓 All ShanghaiTech Students
The course is open to all students across all schools and majors. No prior experience or knowledge in programming, design, robotics, or AI is needed.




🌐 Diversified Teams
We organize groups where different skills and backgrounds complement each other — so every student can contribute meaningfully and learn most effectively.

title: Week 2 — Design
text:
// WEEK TWO  ·  第二周
  Design
  设计
  
    You have a concept. Now make it real — at low fidelity first, then higher.
    Week 2 is about rapid prototyping and fearless iteration:
    electronics on a breadboard, firmware running hello-world, and a 3D sketch of the body.

title: Week 2 — Design
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of Week 2 you will be able to:

Wire a basic ESP32 circuit with a display or sensor
Flash firmware using the ESP-Claw framework
Send your first message to an LLM via OpenRouter and receive a response
Sketch and prototype the robot’s physical form
Run a first user test (lo-fi prototype, real feedback)

title: Week 2 — Design
text:
Sessions

Session 4 — Electronics & Firmware First Contact
Get the hardware talking.






Note



This is often the most intimidating session. Don’t worry — by the end of it your board will be blinking, displaying text, or responding to a button press. That’s real.


Agenda: - Intro to ESP32 GPIO, I2C, and SPI interfaces - Wire up a display (OLED or LCD) on a breadboard - Flash a starter ESP-Claw sketch - Make the display show your robot’s name - Troubleshooting time with instructors
Tools needed:
ESP32 dev board + USB cable
Breadboard + jumper wires
OLED display (SSD1306) or LCD
Arduino IDE or VS Code + PlatformIO



Session 5 — LLM Integration via OpenRouter
Connect your robot’s brain to an AI model.






TipWhy OpenRouter?



OpenRouter provides a single API that routes to any LLM — Qwen, DeepSeek, Claude, and others. You write one integration and can swap models with a single parameter change. No re-wiring required.


Agenda: - OpenRouter account setup + API key - HTTP request from ESP32 to OpenRouter API - Parse JSON response and display reply on screen - Prompt engineering: write a system prompt that gives your robot its personality - Compare two models (e.g. Qwen vs DeepSeek) — what’s different?
Starter prompt template:
You are [robot name], a [personality adjective] AI companion
designed for [target user].

You respond in [language preference], in [tone] — no more than
2 sentences per reply. You never break character.

When someone says hello, you respond with your signature greeting.



Session 6 — Form & Character Development
Guest lecture: JunJun Shi 史珺珺 on “Empathy as the starting point for designing robot personality.”
Agenda: - Guest lecture: how human insight shapes a robot’s soul - Workshop: translate your personality brief into physical form - Sketch 3 body shapes — which one feels like your robot? - Rapid paper prototype: cut, fold, tape — 30 minutes - First user test: show the paper prototype to someone outside your team - Iterate: one change based on feedback






ImportantLo-fi is the point



Paper prototypes aren’t “not done yet.” They’re a tool. The goal is to learn fast and cheaply before committing to 3D printing.




✓ Week 2 Deliverables

Working hardware sketch — ESP32 + display showing robot name / first response
LLM integration demo — robot sends a prompt and displays the reply
3D sketch / paper prototype of the robot’s enclosure
Personality system prompt — documented, tested, iterable
User test notes — what you showed, who you showed it to, what you learned

title: Week 2 — Design
text:
Resources for Week 2

ESP-Claw Getting Started — official docs
OpenRouter API docs — model list, pricing, API reference
Qwen model card — capabilities and prompting tips
DeepSeek documentation — Chinese-language model strengths
Claude API docs — for Claude-specific prompting


← Week 1 — Conceive  |  Week 3 — Build →

title: Week 4 — Pitch It ★
text:
// WEEK FOUR ★  ·  第四周
  Pitch It
  展示
  
    You've built something real. Now tell its story.
    Week 4 is your public debut: a poster, a live demo, and a 5-minute presentation
    to university guests, instructors, and peers.

title: Week 4 — Pitch It ★
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of Week 4 you will be able to:

Communicate a product’s value clearly to a non-technical audience
Create a professional academic poster
Give a confident live demo under pressure
Synthesize 4 weeks of work into a coherent narrative
Receive and respond to critical questions gracefully

title: Week 4 — Pitch It ★
text:
The Final Deliverables
Three things are due on Demo Day:

1. Live Demo (2 min)
A rehearsed, working demo of your robot’s key interaction. Not a video — the robot itself, running live.






ImportantMurphy’s Law



Something will go wrong during the demo. Prepare for it: have a backup power source, know how to reboot quickly, and have a “graceful failure” script ready (“Let me restart — while it boots, let me tell you what it just did…”).





2. Academic Poster (A1 or A0)
Your poster is a standalone artifact that tells the full story of your project. It should be readable without you standing next to it.
Poster structure:



Section
Content
Size




Title
Robot name + one-line description
Large


The Problem
Who is the user? What’s the need?
Medium


Our Solution
What the robot does and how
Medium


Technology
Hardware stack + LLM integration diagram
Medium


Design Process
Key decisions and iterations
Medium


Results
User test findings, what worked
Medium


Lessons Learned
What you’d do differently
Small


QR Code
Links to GitHub repo or demo video
Small









TipPoster design advice




One key image per section — photos of your robot, circuit, or users testing it
Limit text: captions and bullets, not paragraphs
Use your brand colors consistently
Print at least one day early — printers break






3. Pitch Presentation (5 min + Q&A)
A 5-minute structured pitch to university guests.
Recommended structure:
00:00 — Hook (30 sec): Open with the user's problem. Make it vivid.
00:30 — Solution (1 min): Introduce the robot. What does it do?
01:30 — Demo (2 min): Live demonstration. Let the robot speak.
03:30 — Under the hood (45 sec): How it works — ESP32, ESP-Claw, LLM.
04:15 — What we learned (30 sec): One design insight, one technical lesson.
04:45 — Close (15 sec): Who should use this. Why it matters.






NoteQ&A is part of the evaluation



Guests will ask real questions. Don’t be defensive — say “that’s a good question, here’s what we found” or “we don’t know yet, but our hypothesis is…” Intellectual honesty is respected more than false confidence.

title: Week 4 — Pitch It ★
text:
Sessions

Session 10 — Prep & Polish
One session dedicated to hardening the demo, finalizing the poster, and rehearsing the pitch.
Agenda: - Demo run-through × 3 (rotate presenters) - Poster final review — peer feedback, one round of edits - Submit print file for poster - Instructor rehearsal: 5-minute pitch + Q&A practice



Session 11 — Demo Day ★
The main event. University guests, all teams presenting simultaneously (poster session format).
Schedule: - Setup: 30 minutes before guests arrive - Poster session: 90 minutes (guests circulate freely) - Full-group pitches: each team 5 min + 5 min Q&A - Reflection: what would you build next?






TipDuring the poster session



At least one team member should always be at the poster to engage with guests. Rotate so everyone gets to walk around and see other teams’ work.




✓ Final Deliverables (Demo Day)

Working robot demo — live, rehearsed, reliable
Printed A1/A0 poster — follows the poster structure above
5-minute pitch — all team members participate
GitHub repo — clean code, README, photos, short video
Reflection document (1 page) — what you built, what you learned, what’s next




  
    🏆 Four weeks. One product. All yours.
  
  
    Everything you conceive, design, build, and pitch in Maker-X is your work.
    Take it further after the course ends.
  

← Week 3 — Build
